key_insight: Macroscopic elastic loading can drive abnormal grain growth by lowering elastic energy, selectively favoring grains with in-plane <100> orientation aligned to the load.
engineering_relevance: This suggests grain stability in ultrafine-grained metals can depend on elastic anisotropy and loading path, which matters for designing durable thin films and microdevices.
method: Phase-field simulations to track grain growth kinetics under cyclic elastic loading
Micromechanical analysis to link local stress states and grain geometry to driving forces
Extension to materials with different elastic anisotropy to test generality
evidence_signal: The abstract reports simulation results that reproduce the observed abnormal grain growth and show that local geometry and stress state control the growth pathway.
watch_item: The mechanism is inferred primarily from modeling, so experimental validation across more materials, loading amplitudes, and microstructures is still needed.
paper_title: Phase-field modeling of elastically driven abnormal grain growth
